Q: Treated Quant as a strength but still shifted toward harder 805+ practice and deeper conceptual clarity.
Completed the Official Quant book and analyzed every mistake.
Wrote out work with a pen for every question to slow down, capture relevant information, and reduce silly errors.
Practiced hard GMAT Club questions by topic for real-exam readiness, emphasizing accuracy over question count.
Used written work even for graphs and tables so that rushing did not drive avoidable mistakes.
V: Used 1:1 help to slow down, understand RC passages and CR arguments fully, and stop guessing.
Built a read-retain-solve approach so that RC and CR answers did not require constant back-and-forth rereading.
Practiced RC with longer reading material such as Aeon essays.
DI: Practiced DI with Official Online Exclusive questions and GMAT Club DI quizzes.
Improved by spending time understanding each question framework before attacking the answer.
Saved time on Data Sufficiency and tried to spend more time on MSR, but later recognized that this left TPA rushed.
Identified TPA as an underprepared area and recommended practicing it deeply.
Treated DI as manageable when read slowly and processed carefully, with timing as the main enemy.
overall: Restarted prep after an earlier 645 attempt and reached 705 over roughly three months.
Used mentorship for accountability while balancing prep with full-time work.
Shifted from chasing volume to solving difficult questions accurately and reviewing weak topics.
Focused on weak areas despite limited time from work.

V: For RC, invested four to five minutes upfront to understand the passage before moving to questions.
Aimed to understand enough structure and meaning to answer efficiently rather than memorizing every detail.
Used the stronger first read to answer most RC questions quickly afterward.
DI: Improved DI by front-loading the data set before answering questions.
For MSR, spent extra time understanding all sources so that later questions became faster and more accurate.
Treated DI as a section where owning the data matters more than rushing into the first question.
overall: Needed visible structure and benchmarks to stay motivated while preparing around full-time work.
Created a strategy document two weeks before the exam covering hard starts, bad sections, skip decisions, and end-of-section time pressure.
Used a mindset of letting the test play out instead of judging performance mid-section.
Prioritized consistency over intensity, favoring daily work over occasional long sessions.
After a 655 first attempt, changed mainly mindset and test-day execution before scoring 695.

Q: For a strong Quant section, tested first, identified gaps, and then returned to targeted course modules to plug them.
Used Quant improvement to support DI as well, since part of DI is quant-based.
Reviewed error patterns before adding more practice so that weak concepts and recurring mistakes determined the next study focus.
Used wide question exposure and repeated recall through an error log to improve concept recognition under pressure.
V: Started prep with Verbal, then moved to Quant and DI.
For Verbal, learned skills first and then tested them, rather than using the test-first approach applied to Quant.
Used repeated recall at the topic level before sectionals and mocks.
DI: Viewed DI as a combination of Quant and Verbal skills.
Prepared DI by learning the relevant skills first and then testing them.
Connected Quant skill improvement to DI improvement because many DI questions depend on quantitative reasoning.
Built stamina and pacing by recreating full test conditions, including section order and the timed break.
overall: Used a systematic six-month prep cycle with official mocks and e-GMAT.
Followed a daily structure of course modules, testing, and solution review, with review used to identify process gaps.
Emphasized repeated recall over repeated exposure: learn a skill, test it, review it, and repeat at topic level.
Kept a simple notebook error log for wrong answers and for correct answers that took longer than median time.
Discovered that many errors were behavioral rather than knowledge-based, then worked on breaking those patterns.

Q: Used Manhattan Prep Quant Foundation and Quant books to rebuild concepts from scratch.
Solved all Official Guide Quant questions, including the main guide and Quant Review.
Used GMAT Club 700+ Quant questions to stretch reasoning beyond official practice.
Focused on basics and logical reasoning when official Quant became difficult at the high end.
V: Tried Manhattan and PowerScore RC/CR resources but found that heavy question categorization led to overanalysis.
Improved after switching to GMAT Ninja videos, especially by learning what not to do.
Solved Verbal questions from easy to medium to difficult and began spotting patterns and hidden keywords.
Focused on the question at hand rather than spending too much time classifying question types.
DI: Solved all Official Guide DI questions and found them especially valuable because the questions felt unique.
Worked DI questions from easy to medium to difficult until patterns became more recognizable.
Treated official DI practice as closer to the exam than third-party material.
overall: Used the first official attempt as a diagnostic, then prepared from scratch for four months.
During the concept phase, studied before and after work on weekdays and used structured 90-minute weekend sessions.
During the practice phase, solved official questions and reviewed each solution immediately, whether right or wrong.
Avoided taking mocks in the final four to five days before the exam to prevent confidence swings.
After a 675 attempt, focused on mindfulness, breathing, and letting go of score expectations rather than adding more content.

Q: Used free official prep, GMAT Club practice questions, and GMAT Ninja rather than paid resources.
Practiced two sections per day and gradually increased difficulty until 705+ questions could be solved with high accuracy.
Tracked avoidable Quant mistakes and worked specifically on reducing silly errors.
Used GMAT Club to practice under time pressure.
V: Identified Verbal, especially CR, as the hardest section.
Used GMAT Ninja to address weak concepts and improve approach.
Relied on consistent practice and pacing rather than a single shortcut.
Scheduled Verbal last on test day after a break so it could be attempted with a clear mind.
DI: Worked specifically on completing DI on time because getting stuck on one question caused large time losses.
Tracked DI timing and mistakes during mocks.
Started the official exam with DI, followed by Quant, then used the break before Verbal.
overall: Prepared for about two and a half months while working full time, usually studying two to two and a half hours per day.
Used mostly practice and mocks because prior CAT prep provided a starting foundation.
Took free mocks from GMAT Club, e-GMAT, Experts Global, and official prep to track progression.
Maintained notes on timing and mistakes by section, especially avoidable errors.
Took breaks when practice went poorly and returned with better focus instead of forcing low-quality study.

Q: Stopped assuming CAT Quant preparation would transfer directly to GMAT Quant.
Read Quant questions twice before starting calculations to avoid traps and careless misreads.
Added avoided topics such as probability and statistics because every GMAT topic could appear.
Treated GMAT Quant as a precision test rather than a difficulty contest.
V: For CR, stopped relying on conversational English or answer choices that sounded right.
Identified the main conclusion first, then broke down the evidence supporting it before reading answer choices.
Forced a consistent CR process until it became automatic.
For RC, practiced understanding precise meaning rather than only the general idea.
Paused strategically while reading to ensure each sentence was actually understood before moving on.
DI: Accepted that it can be better to leave difficult DI questions than rush the entire section.
Recognized that DI has no partial credit on multi-part questions, so precision mattered more than attempting everything.
Reframed DI as a work-style data problem to reduce intimidation.
Allowed some enjoyment and curiosity in DI instead of treating it as the section that would sink the score.
overall: Moved from a 555 practice test to 715 by learning the GMAT’s mechanics and then building section-specific processes.
Used section order of Verbal, Quant, then DI based on energy demands and strengths.
Built stamina with condensed section-specific practice before full-length tests.
Experimented with section order in practice tests rather than assuming one default order.
Learned that process beats instinct, especially when easy questions can build bad habits.
